title: EU unveils industrial plan to cut reliance on Chinese clean-tech imports
author: Kate Abnett
body:
BRUSSELS — The European Commission on Wednesday presented a new industrial strategy aimed at scaling domestic production of solar panels, batteries and wind turbines, as the bloc seeks to reduce dependence on Chinese manufacturers while meeting its 2030 climate targets.

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said the Green Industrial Compact would unlock 40 billion euros in loans and grants over five years, coordinated with the European Investment Bank and national governments in Germany, France and Poland.

"Europe cannot build its energy transition on a single supplier," von der Leyen told reporters in Brussels. "We need resilient supply chains, skilled workers and factories on European soil."

Climate Commissioner Wopke Hoekstra said the plan would simplify permits for new factories and create a joint procurement platform for member states. Several lawmakers in the European Parliament welcomed the proposal, though Green MEP Bas Eickhout warned that weaker trade defenses could still allow cheap imports to undercut European producers.

German Economy Minister Robert Habeck said Berlin would support two new battery gigafactories in Saxony and Bavaria. French President Emmanuel Macron hosted executives from TotalEnergies and EDF in Paris, calling for a "European preference" in public tenders for renewable infrastructure.

Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk argued that Central Europe should become a manufacturing hub for heat pumps and grid equipment, citing investments near Wrocław and Gdańsk. Critics in Warsaw said the plan did not address rising electricity prices for households in the Mazovia region.

Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson Lin Jian said Beijing opposed discriminatory procurement rules and warned that protectionism would slow the global fight against climate change. U.S. climate envoy John Podesta, visiting Brussels, urged transatlantic cooperation on critical minerals sourced from Chile, Australia and Canada.

Industry groups including WindEurope and SolarPower Europe said the package was a step forward but called for faster grid expansion across the North Sea and Baltic Sea interconnectors. Environmental activists staged a protest outside the Berlaymont building, demanding stronger methane regulations and a firm date to phase out coal in the Western Balkans.

Analysts at BloombergNEF said the plan could reshape competition with China's BYD and CATL, but noted that Europe still lacks enough lithium refining capacity. The Commission said it would present detailed state-aid guidelines in June, ahead of a summit in Copenhagen on energy security.
